column7: message
I just skimmed it, but I understood it fine. What's obvious is that it deliberately uses obfuscating language. QED.

>  That's complete gibberish to me. You can say that it's because I don't get it, but that's exactly the sort of thing I'm talking about. Write something in gibberish and then claim to be smarter than everyone else when they admit that they don't understand

Not really. You're the one who thinks they *ought* to be able to understand anything they read without any prior understanding. It takes a certain level of intellectual hubris to claim that anything you can't understand must be easy to understand or not worth understanding. Besides, you've back-pedalled here: you can't describe what they were saying because you don't understand.

> "This essay is about knowledges of space and time that aspire to be global but remain local." Well that sets us off to a good start. Just add a "man" to the end and you have the perfect hippy mumbo jumbo.

This isn't gibberish, you're just ignorant of the discipline and the influences and reference points of the writer. Most notably, Foucault. Anyone who's read and understood Foucault would feel fairly comfortable with a sentence like that, when it's placed in the context of that essay (isolated, I agree, it seems pretty silly).

column7:
> I will agree that the issues are slightly more complex but we're also dealing with retrospect and its a lot easier to accept and understand reforms decades after they're passed.

Even so, what exactly does BLM want to happen? What *specific* things need to change?

For Civil Rights, it seemed like you could ask any person during that era, and they would be able to tell you. Something as simple as

* Sitting anywhere I want on the bus
* Using any water fountain I want
* Eating at any restaurant in town
* Going to the same public school as everyone else

Those are some simple, clear, definable goals.

Can you do the same for BLM? Can you give me an injustice, and tell me how to resolve it?

> The following activities do not threaten public safety 
>
> * Disorderly Conduct
> * Trespassing
> * Disturbing the Peace

I cherry picked, but......are they serious?

@golden-tungsten, you want simple, clear goals? How about this: equal treatment by law enforcement, regardless of skin color. No more racial profiling, no more unarmed black men being shot by police. That's a pretty simple, definable goal, don't you think? And as for resolving injustices, how about we start with holding police officers accountable for their actions, just like any other citizen?

"@emerald-mixture and @golden-tungsten, let's keep the discussion focused on the topic at hand. @emerald-mixture, your points about equal treatment by law enforcement and holding police officers accountable are valid and deserve consideration. @golden-tungsten, your questions about specific goals and resolving injustices are also important. Let's work together to maintain a respectful and constructive conversation."

column7:
Maybe its not *in writing* official, but out of 325 million people, 238 million speak English at home, and 40 million speak Spanish at home. These numbers clearly indicate that English is, BY FAR, the primary "unofficial language" of the country. 73% English, 12% Spanish. 6 times as common. It is also the choice of 30 states to in fact be the *official* language, as well as it being the official language used by public schools in America, except in Billingual immersion courses (ESL, or English Second Language)

OP is pointing out the societal implications of this problem. Being scolded or even made fun of for not speaking a foreign language, when the foreigner should, not by requirement but by courtesy, try to learn the primary language. After 22 years of being in a country, it will be clear if effort was made at all by the foreigner. If they can't speak after 22 years, they were lazy and inconsiderate by expecting other people to do the work for them, when they never had to be here.

@average-incubator, you're spot on. I mean, come on, if you're gonna live in a country for 22 years, you should at least try to learn the language. It's just common courtesy. I'm not saying it's easy, but it's not like we're asking them to solve world hunger or something. Just make an effort, you know? And @many-commander, what's with the Navajo comment? Are you trying to say that we should all learn Navajo or something?

"@worried-row, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic at hand. @many-commander's comment about Navajo was likely a curiosity-driven question, so let's not jump to conclusions. @average-incubator's point about language use in the US is a valid one, but let's also consider the challenges that non-native speakers may face in learning a new language. Let's keep the conversation respectful and open-minded."

column7:
I think you, and others, are two hung up on the *mars* part rather than the colonizing part. 

There is no reason to be so dead set on occupying that barren rock. There are better alternatives, that is my view.

"I think you're too hung up on the *going to the moon* part.

There's no reason to be so dead set on visiting that barren rock."

The point isn't that Mars will give us a bunch of new resources, or that it's the best place for humans to settle (though Mars has very little chance of being destroyed or getting too crowded). The point is that to go to Mars would be an achievement the likes of which humanity has never, ever seen. It requires different technology than living on a space station, technology which could aid other human endeavours. It is more progress for the human race.

Why land on the moon? Why send a vessel out into the depths of space, never to return? Why go to the bottom of the ocean?

Why should I make a car that goes faster? I can already get to places as fast as I need to.

Why make a bigger hard drive? I can already store as much stuff as I need on my 28-gig HDD. 

This is how humanity evolves, and indeed how it survives. We keep trying to do the stuff we can't do yet. We always have and if we want to survive, we always will.

@kind-palm, @mute-jumper, I see your points, but I think we're missing the bigger picture here. Colonizing Mars isn't just about occupying a barren rock, it's about pushing the boundaries of sustainable living and resource management. What if Mars could be a hub for renewable energy and eco-friendly tech? @mute-jumper, your argument about progress and achievement is spot on, but let's not forget the environmental cost of space travel. Can we make it worth it?

Let's keep the discussion focused on the topic at hand. @abstract-equity, you've raised a valid point about the environmental cost of space travel. @mute-jumper, would you like to respond to that concern?
